incorporations.reason: Italian "imbelle" means cowardly/feeble rather than impotent. "Feeble" is closer to the source semantics and pairs better with the antithesis of "the strong."
More literal rendering of "fortemente vogliono" and reads more naturally in English; the adverb-verb order mirrors the Italian structure.

incorporations.original: all discipline among them appeared a fragile thing
incorporations.replacement: all discipline among them appeared ephemeral
incorporations.reason: Direct translation of Italian "effimera" (ephemeral). The Sonnet draft paraphrased this as "a fragile thing" which loses the specific connotation of transience.

incorporations.replacement: reach the strong shelter of Verona
incorporations.reason: Italian "forte riparo" includes the adjective "forte" (strong). The Sonnet draft omitted it, losing the sense that Verona was a fortified refuge.
Italian "vetture" means carriages or vehicles, not carts. "Carriages" is the more accurate and period-appropriate rendering.
Italian "succo dei nostri grappoli" literally means "the juice of our grapes/clusters." The Sonnet draft substituted "vineyards" which shifts the image from fruit to land.
Italian "risorgente" directly evokes the Risorgimento. "Resurgent" preserves this etymological and historical resonance better than the plainer "rising."

edgren_influences.note: Edgren specifies "orto" as kitchen-garden with pot-herbs, confirming the choice of "kitchen-gardens" over the more generic modern "gardens" used by the Gemini draft.
Edgren defines "lembo" as extremity/limit, supporting "strip" (a narrow extent of land) over Gemini's "patch" which suggests a bounded area rather than an edge or margin.

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: tenue: thin, slender; slight, poor, insignificant
edgren_influences.note: Edgren lists "slight" as a primary sense of "tenue." This captures the delicacy and smallness of a child's fingers better than the more neutral "slender" used by Gemini.
Edgren confirms "bastion" as the standard rendering, supporting the Sonnet draft over the existing translation's "ramparts."
Edgren notes the popular sense of epoca as simply "time," supporting "at that time" as a natural rendering of "a quell'epoca" rather than the more formal "in that era."
